coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible sulfur nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Shi, Huanxian
Yang, Min
Yao, Zhuoru
Zhang, Bin
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Xue, Weiming
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal treatment of EDTA disodium and sodium sulfide. The prepared S,N-CQDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra to characterize their morphology, crystal structure, functional groups, elemental composition, and optical properties. It was found that S and N elements were successfully doped into the CQDs and the morphology was approximately spherical with an average particle size of 2.16 nm, in which the excitation/emission wavelengths were 350 and 420 nm, respectively. Compared with single element doped CQDs, double element doped CQDs have a higher quantum yield and excellent optical stability. Cell experiments showed that S,N-CQDs had good biocompatibility because they had no obvious toxicity on both normal cell lines and cancer cell lines. More importantly, based on the synergy of static quenching and dynamic quenching, the S,N-CQDs were used as effective fluorescent probes for sensitive detection of DA, with high anti-interference and low limit of detection. Based on the good biocompatibility of S,N-CQDs, the detection of dopamine in actual serum samples were carried out and the results showed an excellent recovery rate. Therefore, this work provides a dopamine sensor with a practical application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Detection of dopamine
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of N-CQDs and S,N-CQDs
Fluorescent probe detection of DA based S,N-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of S,N-CQDs. (b) The size distribution histogram of S,N-CQDs. (c) HRTEM of S,N-CQDs. (d) XRD of S,N-CQDs.
(a) FTIR of S,N-CQDs. (b) XPS survey of S,N-CQDs. XPS of C 1s (c), O 1s (d) N 1s (e) and S 2p (f).

(a) The UV absorption, excitation and emission spectra of S,N-CQDs, Inset: photographs of the solution of S,N-CQDs under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (b) The fluorescence spectra of S,N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence lifetime of S,N-CQDs in the presence and absence of DA.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of S,N-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of DA; Insert were the photographs of S,N-CQD aqueous solutions with different concentration of DA under visible light (up) and 365 nm UV lamp365 nm UV lamb. (b) Linear relationship between DA concentration and fluorescence intensity F0/F. (c) The fluorescence response of N-CQDs in the presence of 1 mM DA alone and a mixture of DA with other interferent.
(a) TEM of S,N-CQDs in the presence of DA. (b) The size distribution histogram of S,N-CQDs after agglomeration.
Cell viability of the RAW 264.7 cells and HeLa cells after incubation with the S,N-CQDs for 20 h.
The possible mechanism of S,N-CQDs for detecting DA.
The comparison of the proposed method with other reported methods of DA determination.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible sulfur nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: c College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang, 712046, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: d School of Pharmacy, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang, 712046, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal treatment of EDTA disodium and sodium sulfide. The prepared S,N-CQDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra to characterize their morphology, crystal structure, functional groups, elemental composition, and optical properties. It was found that S and N elements were successfully doped into the CQDs and the morphology was approximately spherical with an average particle size of 2.16 nm, in which the excitation/emission wavelengths were 350 and 420 nm, respectively. Compared with single element doped CQDs, double element doped CQDs have a higher quantum yield and excellent optical stability. Cell experiments showed that S,N-CQDs had good biocompatibility because they had no obvious toxicity on both normal cell lines and cancer cell lines. More importantly, based on the synergy of static quenching and dynamic quenching, the S,N-CQDs were used as effective fluorescent probes for sensitive detection of DA, with high anti-interference and low limit of detection. Based on the good biocompatibility of S,N-CQDs, the detection of dopamine in actual serum samples were carried out and the results showed an excellent recovery rate. Therefore, this work provides a dopamine sensor with a practical application prospect.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), a key neurotransmitter in the hypothalamus and pituitary gland, plays many essential roles in the hormonal, central nervous and cardiovascular systems [1]. It regulates various biological processes such as sleep, memory, and lust [2]. Abnormal levels of dopamine in body may lead to some diseases such as Parkinson's disease [3], schizophrenia [4], attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder [5], Huntington’s disease [6], paragangliomas and pheochromocytomas [7]. Therefore, the quantitative and fast determination of DA is essential. A variety of methods including chromatography [8], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [9], electrochemistry [10], colorimetry [11], micro-dialysis, and voltammetry [12] have been developed to detect DA. However, these methods usually suffer from several drawbacks like prolonged detection time, laborious and time-consuming procedures, professional skills, expensive instruments [13,14], which restrain the widespread application of above traditional detection methods. By contrast, the fluorescence detection method has more appealing advantages, including high sensitivity, simple instrument, facile operation and low cost. These advantages can make it an alternative substitute for traditional detection methods.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of fluorescence nanomaterial, have attracted many research interests due to their unique biocompatibility, high resistance to photobleaching, outstanding optical-stability, and simple preparation process [15–17]. Compared with the traditional semiconductor quantum dots containing heavy metals and organic fluorescent dyes with weak photobleaching resistance, CQDs are undoubtedly the overwhelming candidates for fluorescent probes [18,19]. In order to improve the fluorescence properties of CQDs, heteroatom doping has often been proposed due to that can effectively adjust the intrinsic structure and electronic distribution of CQDs, improve quantum yield, and be beneficial to applications such as bioimaging and bio-detection [20]. Up to now, a large number of heteroatoms doped carbon quantum dots have been reported. The heteroatoms used for doping include metallic elements Zn, Cu, Na, Pt, Yb, Ru, Eu, Au, Ag, Mg, etc. [21–24], as well as nonmetallic elements N, S, P, B, Si, F, I, Se, etc. [25–27]. Among those heteroatoms doping, N-doping and S-doping are the most commonly used strategies to improve the optical and material properties of CQDs. For instance, Qu et al. used folic acid as a carbon source and nitrogen source to prepare the N-CQDs with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) as high as 94.5 % [28]; Xu et al. prepared S-doped CQDs with a QY as high as 67 % by a one-step hydrothermal method and the sodium citrate and sodium thiosulfate as the carbon source and the sulfur source, respectively [29]. Compared with single-heteroatom doping, co-doping multiple heteroatoms will greatly improve the performance of CQDs due to the synergistic effect between the doped heteroatoms [30–32]. At present, although there are some reports about the co-doping of multiple heteroatom CQDs, there are few reports about the co-doping of S and N elements. Moreover, N, S, and C are essential elements in the organism. Therefore, the study of S and N co-doped CQDs is very meaningful for CQDs in biomolecule detection.

In this study, a novel fluorescent probe based on S,N-CQDs for highly selective and sensitive detection of DA were prepared. The S,N-CQDs were synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal method using EDTA disodium and sodium sulfide as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources for the first time. The fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs was quenched in the presence of DA within the concentration range of 0−50 μM with a low limit of detection down of 0.082 μM. The selectivity of DA over the other 17 interferences was also evaluated. Moreover, the cell experiments showed that S,N-CQDs had good biocompatibility which can meet the precondition of actual biological sample detection. Therefore, the fluorescent probe was applied to the detection of DA in real serum samples and good recoveries (96.85 % ∼ 102.72 %) were obtained, which indicated that the as-prepared S,N-CQDs fluorescent probe could effectively and rapidly detect DA in real serum samples.

EDTA disodium was provided by Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Sodium sulfide was obtained from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was collected from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Phosphate buffered solution (pH 7.2–7.4, 0.01 M) was purchased from Quanzhou Hongke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (AR) was purchased from PAI NI Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou, China) provided. Reagents for interference detection were placed in the supplementary material section. Mouse monocyte macrophage (RAW 264.7) cell line was purchased from Cell Bank of Shanghai Chinese Academy of Sciences. RPMI 1640 medium (Bi, Israel) and fetal bovine serum (Bi, Israel) were purchased from Shanghai Xiaopeng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio Life Sciences. Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from Tianjin kemeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. HeLa cell lines and rat serum were provided by Shaanxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Ultrapure water was purified by a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). All of the chemical reagents mentioned were analytical grades without further treatment in this work.
The instrument`s information of work was placed in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of N-CQDs and S,N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of N-CQDs, 2 mmol EDTA disodium was added to 50 ml ultrapure water under ceaselessly stirring for 10 min, then transferred to a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave and held at 200 ℃ for 8 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles, and the supernatant was further purified through a dialysis membrane (500 MW) for 48 h to remove small molecular impurities.
For the synthesis of S,N-CQDs, the first step was to dissolve 2 mmol EDTA disodium and 2 mmol Na2S in 50 mL ultrapure water, and then the next step was the same as the synthesis of N-CQDs.

The fluorescence QY of N-CQDs and S,N-CQDs were determined by a comparison method [33] with quinine sulfate as standard reference as follows:
(1)
φ x
=
φ s
×
G r a d
x
G r a d
s
×
η x
2
η s
2
(2)
G r a d =
I A
Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution, and its QY was 54 % [34].

Where I , η , φ and
A stood for the integral area of fluorescence intensity, refractive index of the solvent, QY, and UV–vis, respectively. G r a d is the slope from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance. The x and s stood for object and reference, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1, the QY of as-prepared N-CQDs and S,N-CQDs was 3.92 % and 7.26 %, respectively.

For detection dopamine, different concentrations of DA stock solutions (0.5 mL) were added to 4.5 ml as-prepared S,N-CQDs, and the final concentration of DA was 0−50 μM, followed by incubation at room temperature for 10 min and then the fluorescence spectra of these solutions were measured and recorded.

The cytotoxicity study of S,N-CQDs was performed on normal cells (RAW 264.7) and cancer cells (Hela cell) by a standard MTT assay. The RAW 264.7 was cultured in complete growth medium containing 85 % RPMI 1640 medium and 15 % fetal bovine serum. The HeLa cell was cultured in a complete growth medium containing 90 % RPMI 1640 medium, 10 % fetal bovine serum, and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. The culture conditions were 37 ℃, 5% CO2 incubator. RAW 264.7 or Hela cells were subcultured into 96 well plates (1 × 105 cells / well) and cultured in the incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 ℃ for 20 h. Then the supernatant was discarded and the control group and the treated group were set up respectively. The control group was added with 100 μL medium, and the treated group was added with 90 μL medium and 10 μL S,N-CQDs of different concentrations, and then cultured in the incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 ℃ for 20 h. Then 20 μL MTT reagent (5 mg/mL) was added into each well, and the supernatant was discarded after 4 h of culture. Each well was dissolved with 150 μL DMSO. After shaking for 5 min, the absorbance was detected at 490 nm. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
(3)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
A b s o r b a n c e
t r e a t e d
A b s o r b a n c e
c o n t r o l
× 100 %
Three parallel tests were conducted out in three generations of cells to prove the credibility of the experiment.

Rat serum samples were diluted 100 times using PBS buffer solution (0.01 M, pH = 7.2–7.4). DA of known concentration was added into the above rat serum sample. Then the serum solution containing different concentrations of DA was added to the S,N-CQDs solution, incubated for 10 min, and the fluorescence spectrum of the mixed solution was measured and recorded.

In the previous work [35], it was found that sodium sulfide can be used as a high-quality sulfur source. Therefore, sodium sulfide was still used as sulfur source in this work. As shown in Scheme S1, the S,N-CQDs were prepared by using EDTA disodium and sodium sulfide through one-pot hydrothermal method. Because different reaction conditions can affect the fluorescence properties of CQDs by affecting the crystallinity, surface defects and size of CQDs [36]. To obtain S,N-CQDs with excellent optical properties, the following reaction conditions have been optimized: molar ratio of EDTA disodium and Na2S (Fig. S2a), reaction temperature (Fig. S2b) and reaction time (Fig. S2c).

As shown in Fig. S2a, with the increase of the ratio of sodium sulfide, the fluorescence emission peak of prepared CQDs gradually red shifted, and the fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased. The redshift may be due to the lower electronegativity of sulfur, which can absorb lower energy light and emit longer wavelength light [37,38]. However, when the ratio of sulfur is too high, it may lead to the increase of surface defects of CQDs and then lead to the non-radiation transition of excited electrons, and finally reduce the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. As shown in Fig. S2b and S2c, increasing the reaction temperature and time can make the precursor reactant fully and increase the yield of CQDs, and thus enhance the fluorescence intensity. The results showed that the optimal molar ratio, optimal reaction temperature, and optimal reaction time of reactants were 1:1, 200 ℃ and 8 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2a, compared with the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the S,N-CQDs was nearly doubled. And the quantum yield of S,N-CQDs is 1.85 times higher than that of N-CQDs (Fig. S1). This may be due to the fact that double element doping is more conducive to decreasing and improving the surface defects of CQDs and increasing the electron radiation transition probability of CQDs [39,40].

The microscopic morphology and surface functional groups of S,N-CQDs prepared under optimal preparation conditions were characterized by TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS. The TEM (Fig. 1 a) of carbon quantum dots clearly showed that S,N-CQDs were approximately spherical and uniformly dispersed. The average particle size (Fig. 1b) was 2.16 nm with a narrow size distribution ranging from 1.1 nm to 3.0 nm. The high-resolution TEM image of S,N-CQDs showed the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of crystalline graphite (Fig. 1c) [41]. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) results (Fig. S3) showed that the particle size distribution of S,N-CQDs was 1.49–2.69 nm, and the average particle size was 2.03 nm, which confirmed the TEM result. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) showed a broad diffraction peak at 23.54°, which was consistent with the (100) lattice spacing of the carbon-based materials [42]. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of S,N-CQDs (Fig. 2 a) showed the broad bands appeared at 3390 cm−1 for S,N-CQDs were corresponding to OH stretching vibrations, and the characteristic band at 1600 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1 were assigned to COOH and CN vibration, respectively [43]. These hydrophilic functional groups endowed S,N-CQDs good water solubility, which was conducive to the application of S,N-CQDs in the biological field. Although there was no obvious characteristic band of CS or SOx groups in the FT-IR spectra of S, N-CQDs, XPS spectra was further applied to confirm the incorporation of sulfur into the structure of S,N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, survey spectrum of XPS revealed that the S,N-CQDs mainly consist of carbon (C 1s, 284.6 eV, 57.47 %), oxygen (O 1s, 531.1 eV, 27.16 %), nitrogen (N 1s, 399.58 eV, 6.16 %), sulfur (S 2p, 163.22 eV, 4.49 %) and sodium (Na 1s, 1071.17 eV, 4.72 %) [44,45]. The signal of sodium originated from the residue of EDTA disodium. The high-resolution XPS were demonstrated to the combination modes of C, O, N and S in S,N-CQDs. The three peaks of C 1s can be attributed to CC or CC (284.7 eV), CN or CS (285.9 eV), and CO (288.3 eV), respectively (Fig. 2c) [46]. The three peaks of O 1s spectrum can be attributed to CO (531.3 eV), CO (532.2 eV) and OH (535.7 eV) groups, respectively (Fig. 2d) [47]. The N 1s spectrum in Fid. 2e exhibited two peaks at 399.8 eV and 401.7 eV, attributed to CNC and NH, respectively [48]. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum in Fig. 2f exhibited four peaks at 163.2 eV, 164.4 eV, 168 eV and 169.2 eV, corresponded to the S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2, S-O3, and S-O4, respectively [49]. The XPS and FTIR results showed that the S and N atoms have been successfully doped into the S,N-CQDs.

The optical properties of the as-prepared S,N-CQDs were investigated by UV absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the S,N-CQDs exhibited strong absorption at 236 nm and 325 nm, which was ascribed to the π-π* transitions and n-π* transitions of S,N-CQDs, respectively [50]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of S,N-CQDs was around 350 nm and 420 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the colorless aqueous S,N-CQDs solution exhibited strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3a, insert). Additionally, the S,N-CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior (Fig. 3b), that was, the maximum emission peak of S,N-CQDs shifted to a longer wavelength and the PL intensity of S,N-CQDs decreases when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 420 nm. The excitation-dependent emission behavior was consistent with the previously reported [51,52]. This behavior may be attributed to the different emission centers of S,N-CQDs [53,54]. As we all known that the fluorescence lifetime decay function of CQDs with multiple emission centers is generally multi-exponential decay [55]. Therefore, the fluorescence decay of the S,N-CQDs was measured to confirm this speculation. As shown in Fig. 3c, the results of the measure were shown in Table S1, the results found that it was multiple exponential decay with an average lifetime of 6.21 ns, which confirmed the opinion of multiple launch centers. In order to investigate the optical stability of S,N-CQDs, the experiments of light bleaching resistance (Fig. S4a), acid and alkali resistance (Fig. S4b), and salt resistance (Fig. S4c) were carried out. The results found that the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs had no obvious change in different pH solutions, salt solutions and after continuous excitation at 365 nm UV light for 10 h. It was proved that S,N-CQDs have excellent optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent probe detection of DA based S,N-CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: For investigated the mechanism of S,N-CQDs detection DA, the TEM image and fluorescence lifetime decay curve of S, N-CQD quenched by DA were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 , the S,N-CQDs had obvious agglomeration when DA was present in S,N-CQDs solution, with a size distribution of 54 nm–110 nm and an average particle size of 82 nm. The reason for this phenomenon may be due to the agglomeration was occurred of S,N-CQDs agglomerate induced by the noncovalent interaction such as electrostatic interactions, π-π stacking, and hydrogen bonding, which made the because of the hydroxyl, amino groups, and phenyl in DA and the hydroxyl groups, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of the S,N-CQDs [62]. It is widely believed that the fluorescence quenching caused by the formation of complexes belongs to static quenching [63], to prove whether the quenching in our work was static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime was measured. As shown in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence lifetime of S,N-CQDs was very different when dopamine was absent or present, which proved that the quenching process was dynamic quenching. The dynamic quenching is generally caused by electron transfer [63]. Further analysis of the possible reason was that DA molecules adsorbed on the surface of S,N-CQDs were oxidized to DA quinone, and DA quinone can accept the electrons from the photo-excited S,N-CQDs, which leads to the fluorescence quenching of S,N-CQDs [64,65]. Therefore, as shown in Scheme 1 , the quenching mechanism can be summarized as the synergy of static quenching and dynamic quenching.

For the detection of real biological samples, the cytotoxicity of fluorescent probes is a very important factor. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of S,N-CQDs was evaluated by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 6 , the results of the MTT assay showed that the cell viability did not decrease significantly after adding S,N-CQDs (up to 400 μg/mL) into normal cell line (RAW 264.7) and cancer cell line (Hela), indicating that S,N-CQDs possessed low cytotoxicity for cancer and normal cell lines.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed, the concentrate of DA in real samples (healthy rat serum) was detected. The results obtained by the standard addition method were shown in Table S2. The satisfactory recoveries of DA in rat serum were achieved, which indicated that serum did not affect the results of fluorescent probe detection DA, and it had potential application in real sample detection DA.

In summary, a fluorescent probe for detecting dopamine based on S,N-CQDs was successfully prepared. A possible fluorescence quenching mechanism combining static quenching and dynamic quenching was proposed, which is based on the agglomeration and electron transfer of S,N-CQDs. The as-prepared S,N-CQDs not only have higher quantum yield, but also have excellent detection performance for DA, compared N-CQDs. Furthermore, the as-synthesized S,N-CQDs showed low cytotoxicity to normal and cancer cells and can be used as excellent fluorescence probes for biomolecules. In addition, the DA spiked detection of rat serum samples also obtained good recovery.
And, the biomolecular fluorescent probe provided by this work has great practical application potential.

Cunjin Wang: Writing - original draft. Huanxian Shi: Investigation. Min Yang: Conceptualization. Zhuoru Yao: Investigation. Bin Zhang: Investigation. Enzhou Liu: Methodology. Xiaoyun Hu: Funding acquisition. Weiming Xue: Supervision. Jun Fan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2021.111874.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor for dopamine detection
Spectrochim. Acta A. Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

Mater. Res. Bull.
